lists.title: Russian A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in TORFL-elementary order: gender and 'это' sentences first, then present-tense verbs, the first cases (prepositional for place, accusative for objects), possessives, past tense, and the genitive of absence.

lists.title: Russian A2 Grammar Path
lists.description: A2 grammar in TORFL-base order: the dative and instrumental cases, the aspect system (imperfective/perfective), both futures, the first verbs of motion, the imperative, reflexive verbs, the animate accusative, and everyday time expressions.

lists.title: Russian B1 Grammar Path
lists.description: B1 grammar in TORFL-first order: comparison, short adjectives, свой, numerals with case, dates, the conditional, relative который, purpose and reported clauses, impersonal constructions, and prefixed verbs of motion.

lists.title: Russian B2 Grammar Path
lists.description: B2 grammar in TORFL-second order: aspect choice in commands, себя and сам, the four participles, both gerunds, the verbal prefix system, and negative pronouns with double negation.

lists.title: Russian C1 Grammar Path
lists.description: C1 grammar in TORFL-third order: participial style, the general-factual imperfective, чтобы-clauses with a second subject, the reflexive passive, and the declension of complex numerals.

lists.title: Russian C2 Grammar Path
lists.description: C2 discourse-level grammar: formal participial and nominal style, aspect nuance in context (annulled results), discourse particles, and emphatic focus constructions.

points.title: Gender of nouns (он, она, оно)
points.function: Refer back to people and things
points.explanation: Every noun is masculine, feminine, or neuter — usually visible in the ending: consonant = masculine (дом), -а/-я = feminine (книга), -о/-е = neuter (окно). The pronoun that replaces a noun matches its gender: Где дом? — Он там. Где книга? — Она на столе.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — gender

drills.translation: Where is the book? — It is on the table.
Where is the house? — It is there.
Where is the window? — It is there.
Where is mum? — She is at home.
Where is the magazine? — It is on the table.
Where is the sea? — It is far away.

related.title: Past tense (-л)
related.contrast: The past tense agrees in gender too: он читал, она читала.

points.title: Это — this is / it is
points.function: Introduce and identify things
points.explanation: 'Это' plus a noun makes a complete sentence — Russian drops 'to be' in the present: Это дом (This is a house), Это мой друг (This is my friend). Negate with не: Это не школа.
points.culture_note: No articles and no present-tense 'to be': 'Я студент' is literally 'I student' — and completely correct.

references.title: Wikipedia: Russian grammar
Wiktionary: это (Russian)

drills.translation: This is my friend.
This is a school.
This is my sister.
This is our house.
Who is this? — This is my friend Ivan.
This is not water.

related.title: Present tense (читать, говорить)
related.contrast: Russian drops 'to be' in the present: это дом — no verb at all.

related.title: Gender of nouns (он, она, оно)
related.contrast: Это never changes — the noun keeps its own gender.

points.title: Present tense (читать, говорить)
points.function: Talk about what people do
points.explanation: First conjugation (читать): читаю, читаешь, читает, читаем, читаете, читают. Second conjugation (говорить): говорю, говоришь, говорит, говорим, говорите, говорят. The ending alone tells you the person, so pronouns are often kept for clarity but the verb does the work.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian conjugation

drills.translation: I am reading a book.
You speak Russian well.
He speaks English.
We are reading a magazine.
They live in Moscow.
Do you speak Russian?

related.title: Past tense (-л)
related.contrast: Present marks person (я читаю); past marks gender (читал/читала).

related.title: Это — this is / it is
related.contrast: Identity sentences skip the verb; actions conjugate.

points.title: Prepositional case (location: в / на)
points.function: Say where something is
points.explanation: The prepositional case marks location after в (in) and на (on). It only ever appears after a preposition, which is why it is taught first among the cases: в Москве (in Moscow), на столе (on the table). Singular endings are usually -е.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — prepositional

drills.translation: The book is on the table.
I live in Moscow.
We live in Russia.
He works at a school.
Mum works in a shop.
We are talking about the film.

related.title: Accusative case (direct object)
related.contrast: В + prepositional = location; в + accusative = motion into.

related.title: Genitive of absence (у меня нет …)
related.contrast: Where something IS takes prepositional; that it ISN'T takes genitive.

points.title: Accusative case (direct object)
points.function: Say what the action is done to
points.explanation: The direct object takes the accusative. Feminine -а becomes -у (книга → книгу, газета → газету); masculine inanimate nouns don't change (читаю журнал). Animate masculine nouns borrow the genitive form — that contrast comes at A2.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — accusative
Wiktionary: Russian nouns (declension)

drills.translation: I am reading a book.
She is writing a letter.
He is reading a newspaper.
She loves music.
I am drinking water.
We are watching a film.

related.title: Prepositional case (location: в / на)
related.contrast: Accusative for the thing acted on; prepositional for where.

related.title: Genitive of absence (у меня нет …)
related.contrast: Negated existence switches to genitive: нет книги, not книгу.

points.title: Possessive pronouns (мой, моя, моё)
points.function: Say whose something is
points.explanation: Possessives agree with the gender of the THING owned, not the owner: мой дом (m), моя книга (f), моё окно (n), мои книги (pl). The same pattern covers твой (your), наш (our), ваш (your, formal/plural).

references.title: Wikipedia: Russian grammar
Wiktionary: мой (Russian)

drills.translation: This is my book.
This is my house.
This is my window.
These are my books.
Where is your brother?
This is our school.

related.title: Gender of nouns (он, она, оно)
related.contrast: The noun's gender picks the form: мой дом, моя книга.

points.title: Past tense (-л)
points.function: Talk about what happened
points.explanation: Drop -ть and add -л, then agree with the SUBJECT's gender: он читал, она читала, оно читало, они читали. Even 'to be' reappears in the past: он был, она была, они были.
points.culture_note: The past tense agrees in gender, not person — a historical participle. A woman says 'я читала', a man 'я читал'.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian conjugation — past

drills.translation: She was in Moscow.
He watched a film.
I was at home yesterday (male speaker).
We were in the park.
She was reading a book in the evening.
They were watching TV.
That was a long time ago.

related.title: Present tense (читать, говорить)
related.contrast: Past agrees with WHO's gender; present with WHO's person.

related.title: Gender of nouns (он, она, оно)
related.contrast: The -л ending takes -а/-о by the subject's gender.

points.title: Genitive of absence (у меня нет …)
points.function: Say what you have and don't have
points.explanation: Possession is 'у + person + есть': У меня есть брат (I have a brother). Absence is 'нет + genitive': У меня нет брата (I don't have a brother — брат takes genitive -а). This у-construction is the everyday way to say 'have'; the verb иметь is bookish.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — genitive
Wiktionary: нет (Russian)

drills.translation: I have no time.
She doesn't have a car.
Today I have no money.
He doesn't have a job.
We have no milk.
There is no metro in the city.

related.title: Accusative case (direct object)
related.contrast: Having acts on accusative; lacking demands genitive.

related.title: Prepositional case (location: в / на)
related.contrast: Нет says it isn't there; prepositional says where 'there' is.

points.title: Dative case (indirect object)
points.function: Say to whom or for whom something is done
points.explanation: The dative marks the receiver: даю брату книгу (I give my brother a book). Masculine and neuter singular take -у/-ю (брат → брату, окно → окну), feminine -а/-я becomes -е (сестра → сестре). Some everyday verbs always take the dative: помогать (help), звонить (call), отвечать (answer): Я помогаю маме. Pronouns: мне, тебе, ему, ей, нам, вам, им.
points.culture_note: Age is expressed with the dative: Мне двадцать лет — literally 'to me twenty years'.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — dative

drills.translation: I am giving my brother a book.
She helps her mother.
We call our grandmother every day.
I am twenty years old.
drills.hint: age takes the dative of я

drills.translation: He is writing a letter to a friend.
You are answering the teacher's question.

points.title: Мне нравится — liking with the dative
points.function: Say what you like and don't like
points.explanation: Liking flips the sentence around: the liker goes in the dative and the liked thing is the grammatical subject. Мне нравится этот фильм (I like this film — literally 'to me pleases this film'). The verb agrees with the THING: Ей нравятся книги (she likes books — plural нравятся). Past: Нам понравился концерт.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: нравиться (Russian)

drills.translation: I like this film.
She likes books.
I like the Russian language.
Do you like this music?
We like this city.
They like these songs.

points.title: Instrumental case (with, by means of)
points.function: Say with whom, with what, or as what
points.explanation: The instrumental marks the tool or companion. Endings: masculine/neuter -ом/-ем (карандаш → карандашом), feminine -ой/-ей (ручка → ручкой). With с it means 'together with': Я иду в кино с другом. Without a preposition it is the instrument: Я пишу ручкой (I write with a pen). After стать/быть it marks a role: Он хочет стать врачом (He wants to become a doctor). Заниматься takes the instrumental: заниматься спортом.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — instrumental

drills.translation: I write with a pen.
She is going to the cinema with a friend.
He wants to become a doctor.
We do sports.
I drink tea with milk.
She is talking with her sister on the phone.

points.title: Aspect pairs: imperfective and perfective
points.function: Distinguish a process from a completed result
points.explanation: Almost every Russian verb comes as a pair: imperfective for process, repetition, or the activity itself (читать, писать, делать), perfective for a single completed result (прочитать, написать, сделать). In the past this is the key contrast: Вчера я читал книгу (spent time reading) vs Вчера я прочитал книгу (finished it). When you learn a verb, learn its partner with it — vocabulary cards here show both forms together.
points.culture_note: There is no perfect/progressive tense system — aspect does that work. Learners who master the pair habit early save themselves years of confusion.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Grammatical aspect in Slavic languages

drills.translation: Yesterday I finally finished reading this book.
She was writing a letter all evening.
He has already done his homework.
Every day I read the newspaper.

drills.sentence: Она {{answer}} письмо и пошла на почту.
drills.translation: She wrote the letter and went to the post office.
drills.hint: finished before the next action — perfective
drills.transliteration: Ona ___ pis'mo i poshla na pochtu.

drills.translation: We watched this film for a long time.

points.title: Future with буду (imperfective future)
points.function: Talk about ongoing or repeated future actions
points.explanation: The imperfective future is буду/будешь/будет/будем/будете/будут + the imperfective infinitive: Завтра я буду работать (Tomorrow I will be working). Use it for processes, plans without a finish line, and repeated future actions: Я буду читать каждый день.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian conjugation — future

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will be working at home.
What will you be doing in the evening?
We are going to live in Moscow.

drills.sentence: Она будет {{answer}} русский язык каждый день.
drills.translation: She will study Russian every day.
drills.transliteration: Ona budet ___ russkij yazy'k kazhdy'j den'.

drills.translation: They will be watching a film in the evening.
Will you be working tomorrow?

points.title: Perfective future (прочитаю, напишу)
points.function: Promise or predict a completed result
points.explanation: A perfective verb in present-tense endings IS the future — there is no present for perfectives: Я прочитаю эту книгу за неделю (I will finish this book in a week), Я тебе позвоню (I'll call you). Contrast: буду читать (will spend time reading) vs прочитаю (will get it read).

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Grammatical aspect in Slavic languages

drills.translation: I will call you in the evening.
Tomorrow she will write the letter.
I will finish this book in a week.
We will get everything done tomorrow.
Will you show me this photo?
He will buy bread at the shop.

points.title: Verbs of motion: идти vs ходить
points.function: Say where someone is going on foot
points.explanation: Going on foot uses TWO verbs: идти for one direction right now (Сейчас я иду в школу — I'm on my way to school) and ходить for repeated trips or motion in general (Каждый день я хожу в школу — I go to school every day). Past ходил(а) also means a completed round trip: Вчера мы ходили в кино (we went to the cinema — and came back). Destination takes в/на + accusative.
points.culture_note: This one-way/many-ways split runs through all Russian motion verbs — master it here and the rest of the family follows the same logic.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian verbs of motion

drills.translation: I am walking to school right now.

drills.sentence: Каждый день я {{answer}} на работу пешком.
drills.translation: Every day I walk to work.
drills.transliteration: Kazhdy'j den' ya ___ na rabotu peshkom.

drills.translation: Yesterday we went to the cinema.
Where are you going? — To the shop.
She often goes to the theatre.
drills.hint: repeated trips — the multidirectional verb, she

drills.translation: Look, here comes our teacher!

points.title: Verbs of motion: ехать vs ездить
points.function: Say where someone is going by vehicle
points.explanation: Motion by vehicle mirrors идти/ходить: ехать for one direction now (Мы едем в Москву — we're on our way to Moscow) and ездить for repeated or general trips (Каждое лето мы ездим на море). Conjugation: еду, едешь, едет…; езжу, ездишь, ездит… The means of transport takes на + prepositional: ехать на поезде, на автобусе.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian verbs of motion

drills.translation: We are on our way to Moscow now.
Every summer they go to the seaside.
I go to work by bus.
Where are you going? — To the station.

drills.sentence: В прошлом году мы {{answer}} в Петербург.
drills.translation: Last year we went to Saint Petersburg.
drills.transliteration: V proshlom godu my' ___ v Peterburg.

drills.translation: He is going home by taxi.

points.title: Imperative (читай! говорите!)
points.function: Tell someone to do something
points.explanation: Form the imperative from the present stem: vowel stem + -й (читаю → читай), consonant stem + -и when stressed (говорю → говори). Add -те for вы: читайте, говорите. Common commands: Слушай(те)! Смотри(те)! Скажи(те), пожалуйста…
points.culture_note: Adding пожалуйста or the -те form softens a command — bare imperatives to strangers sound abrupt.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian conjugation — imperative

drills.translation: Please tell me, where is the metro?
Read this text at home.
Listen to me carefully.
Look, what a beautiful sky!
Please give me your pen.
Speak Russian, please.

points.title: Reflexive verbs (-ся)
points.function: Talk about study, daily routines, and events
points.explanation: Verbs in -ся conjugate normally and keep the particle: заниматься → занимаюсь, занимаешься; учиться → учусь, учишься. After a vowel -ся becomes -сь: занимаюсь, but занимался. Many everyday verbs are reflexive without any 'self' meaning: урок начинается (the lesson begins), магазин закрывается (the shop closes).

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: -ся (Russian)

drills.translation: I study the Russian language.
She studies at the university.
The lesson begins at nine o'clock.
The shop closes at eight o'clock.
Where do you study? — At school.
We do sports on Saturdays.

points.title: Animate accusative (вижу брата)
points.function: Talk about people as objects of an action
points.explanation: Masculine nouns that name living beings borrow the GENITIVE form in the accusative: Я вижу брата (I see my brother), Она любит Ивана. Inanimate masculines stay unchanged (читаю журнал). Feminine nouns keep their own accusative (-у) whether animate or not: Я вижу сестру.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Animacy — in Slavic languages

drills.translation: I see my brother every day.
She knows the teacher well.
We are waiting for the doctor at the entrance.
Do you love Ivan?
drills.hint: the name — animate, borrows the genitive

drills.translation: I see my sister in the window.
He reads a magazine on the train.

points.title: Time expressions (в понедельник, утром)
points.function: Say when something happens
points.explanation: Days take в + accusative: в понедельник, в среду (on Monday, on Wednesday). Months take в + prepositional: в январе, в мае. Parts of the day and seasons use the bare instrumental: утром, днём, вечером, ночью; зимой, летом. Weeks use на + prepositional: на этой неделе (this week).

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar

drills.translation: We have a lesson on Monday.
My birthday is in January.
In the morning I drink coffee.
In summer we go to the seaside.
In the evening she watches TV.
On Wednesday we go to the swimming pool.

points.title: Comparatives (интереснее, лучше)
points.function: Compare people and things
points.explanation: The simple comparative adds -ее (or -ей): интересный → интереснее, красивый → красивее. A few are irregular and essential: хороший → лучше, плохой → хуже, большой → больше, маленький → меньше, старый → старше. Compare with чем: Москва больше, чем Тверь — or put the compared noun in the genitive: Москва больше Твери.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — adjectives

drills.translation: This book is more interesting than the film.
The weather today is better than yesterday.
Moscow is bigger than Tver.

drills.sentence: Мой брат {{answer}} меня на два года.
drills.translation: My brother is two years older than me.
drills.transliteration: Moj brat ___ menya na dva goda.

drills.translation: In winter the days are shorter than in summer.
Speak more slowly, please.

points.title: Superlatives (самый)
points.function: Single out the best, biggest, or most important
points.explanation: The everyday superlative is самый + adjective, agreeing like any adjective: самый большой город (the biggest city), самая красивая площадь, самое высокое здание. 'Best' is лучший or самый лучший.
points.culture_note: Bookish superlatives in -ейший/-айший (интереснейший, величайший) exist — you'll meet them in reading at higher levels.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: самый (Russian)

drills.translation: Moscow is the biggest city in Russia.
This is the most beautiful square in the city.
Baikal is the deepest lake in the world.
These are the most interesting books in the library.
He is my very best friend.
Summer is the warmest season of the year.

points.title: Short-form adjectives (рад, готов, должен)
points.function: Describe temporary states and obligations
points.explanation: Some adjectives have a short predicate form used only after 'to be': рад/рада/рады (glad), готов/готова/готовы (ready), занят/занята/заняты (busy), болен/больна (ill), должен/должна/должны (must, owe). They agree in gender and number: Я рад тебя видеть. Она готова. Мы должны работать.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — short adjectives

drills.translation: I am very glad to see you (male speaker).
She is already ready for the exam.
We have to work on Saturday.
Sorry, I am very busy right now (male speaker).

drills.sentence: Он не пришёл, потому что он {{answer}}.
drills.translation: He didn't come because he is ill.
drills.transliteration: On ne prishyol, potomu chto on ___.

drills.translation: You must help me (to a male).

points.title: Свой — one's own
points.function: Refer back to the subject's own things
points.explanation: Свой replaces мой/твой/его/её/наш/их when the owner is the SUBJECT of the sentence: Я люблю свою работу (I love my own job), Она читает свою книгу (her own book). Compare: Она читает её книгу = someone else's book. Свой declines like мой: свой, своя, своё, свои.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: свой (Russian)

drills.translation: I love my (own) job.
He talks about his (own) family.
We invited our (own) friends.
She gave me her (own) phone number.
Did you take your (own) documents?
Each person talked about their own city.

points.title: Numerals + case (два часа, пять лет)
points.function: Count things correctly
points.explanation: After 1 the noun is nominative singular (один год). After 2, 3, 4 — genitive SINGULAR: два года, три брата, четыре книги (две for feminine). After 5 and above — genitive PLURAL: пять лет, шесть книг, десять рублей. Compound numbers follow their last word: двадцать один год, двадцать два года, двадцать пять лет.
points.culture_note: Prices and ages run on this system constantly — рубль/рубля/рублей is the everyday drill.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — numerals

drills.translation: I am twenty-two years old.
She is thirty years old.
This costs five roubles.
I have two brothers.
There are four books on the table.
The film runs for three hours.

points.title: Ordinals and dates (первое января)
points.function: Name dates and say when something happened
points.explanation: Dates use the NEUTER ordinal (agreeing with число, 'date'): Сегодня первое января (Today is the first of January) — the month goes in the genitive. 'On a date' puts the ordinal in the genitive too: Я родился второго марта (I was born on the second of March). Years use в + prepositional: в две тысячи двадцатом году.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — ordinal numerals

drills.translation: Today is the fifth of May.
The New Year begins on the first of January.
She was born on the second of March.
Today is the tenth of September.
The exam will be on the fifteenth of June.
This is my first Russian lesson.

points.title: Conditional бы
points.function: Talk about hypothetical and polite wishes
points.explanation: The conditional is simply past tense + бы: Я хотел бы кофе (I would like a coffee), Без тебя мы бы не справились. Hypothetical conditions take если бы in both halves: Если бы у меня было время, я бы пришёл (If I had time, I would come). Бы usually follows the verb or the word it highlights.
points.culture_note: Я хотел(а) бы… is the polite way to order or request — the Russian 'I would like'.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: бы (Russian)

drills.translation: I would like a cup of coffee.

drills.sentence: Если бы у меня было время, я бы {{answer}}.
drills.translation: If I had time, I would come (male speaker).
drills.transliteration: Esli by' u menya by'lo vremya, ya by' ___.

drills.translation: She would gladly come.
She would definitely help you.

drills.sentence: Мы хотели бы {{answer}} билета на вечер.
drills.translation: We would like two tickets for the evening.
drills.transliteration: My' hoteli by' ___ bileta na vecher.

drills.sentence: На твоём месте я бы {{answer}} врачу.
drills.translation: In your place I would call the doctor (male speaker).
drills.transliteration: Na tvoyom meste ya by' ___ vrachu.

points.title: Relative clauses: который
points.function: Describe things with a full clause
points.explanation: Который agrees with its noun in gender and number, but its CASE comes from its role in the relative clause: Вот дом, в котором я живу (the house I live in — prepositional after в). Девушка, которая работает здесь (who works here — subject, nominative). Книга, которую я читаю (which I'm reading — direct object, accusative).

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: который (Russian)

drills.translation: Here is the book that I am reading.
This is the girl who works at the bank.
Here is the house in which I live.
This is the friend whom I called yesterday.
The film that we watched was boring.
The people who live here are very kind.

points.title: Purpose clauses: чтобы + infinitive
points.function: Say why you do something
points.explanation: When the same person does both actions, purpose is чтобы + infinitive: Я учу русский, чтобы работать в Москве (I'm learning Russian in order to work in Moscow). After verbs of motion, чтобы is often optional but always correct: Мы пришли, чтобы поговорить.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: чтобы (Russian)

drills.sentence: Я учу русский, {{answer}} работать в Москве.
drills.translation: I am learning Russian in order to work in Moscow.
drills.transliteration: Ya uchu russkij, ___ rabotat' v Moskve.

drills.translation: We came to have a talk with you.
She got up early so as not to be late.

drills.sentence: Он поехал в магазин, чтобы {{answer}} продукты.
drills.translation: He went to the shop to buy groceries.
drills.transliteration: On poehal v magazin, chtoby' ___ produkty'.

drills.translation: To speak Russian well, you need practice.
I called to find out the schedule.

points.title: Reported speech (что, ли)
points.function: Report what someone said or asked
points.explanation: Statements are reported with что, and the original tense is KEPT: Он сказал, что придёт завтра (He said he would come — Russian keeps the future). Questions with a question word keep it: Она спросила, где я живу. Yes/no questions use ли after the verb: Он спросил, приду ли я.
points.culture_note: No tense backshift — a relief after English. What was said is what is reported.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: ли (Russian)

drills.translation: He said that he would come tomorrow.
She asked where I live.

drills.sentence: Он спросил, приду {{answer}} я на вечер.
drills.translation: He asked whether I would come to the party.
drills.hint: yes/no question → particle after the verb
drills.transliteration: On sprosil, pridu ___ ya na vecher.

drills.sentence: Мама сказала, что она {{answer}} нас вечером.
drills.translation: Mum said that she is waiting for us in the evening.
drills.hint: no tense backshift — present stays present
drills.transliteration: Mama skazala, chto ona ___ nas vecherom.

drills.translation: I don't know what he works as.
She asked whether I speak Russian.
drills.hint: yes/no question → particle after the verb

points.title: Impersonal constructions (надо, можно, нельзя)
points.function: Express necessity, permission, and prohibition
points.explanation: These sentences have no grammatical subject — the person, if named, goes in the dative: Мне надо идти (I have to go), Здесь можно курить? (May one smoke here?), Нельзя опаздывать (One mustn't be late). States work the same way: Мне холодно (I'm cold), Ему скучно. Past adds было: Надо было позвонить.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Impersonal verb

drills.translation: I have to go home.
Is it allowed to take photos here?
One mustn't talk loudly in the library.
I'm cold — close the window, please.
We need to buy tickets in advance.
He is bored — the film isn't interesting at all.

points.title: Prefixed verbs of motion (прийти, уйти, выйти)
points.function: Describe arrivals, departures, and passing through
points.explanation: Prefixes turn motion verbs into precise events: при- arrival (прийти/приходить — Он пришёл домой в семь), у- leaving for good (уйти/уходить), вы- exiting (выйти из комнаты), в- entering (войти), под- approaching (подойти к окну), пере- crossing (перейти улицу). Each prefixed pair keeps the aspect system: прийти is perfective, приходить imperfective.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian verbs of motion — prefixed

drills.translation: He came home at seven o'clock.
She went out of the room.
The director has already left — come back tomorrow.
Come up to the board, please.
You can cross the street here.

drills.sentence: Каждое утро он {{answer}} на работу в восемь.
drills.translation: Every morning he arrives at work at eight.
drills.transliteration: Kazhdoe utro on ___ na rabotu v vosem'.

points.title: Aspect in the imperative and prohibitions
points.function: Command, invite, and forbid with the right nuance
points.explanation: Perfective imperatives request one completed act: Напиши мне! Купи хлеба! Imperfective imperatives invite, encourage a process, or set a routine: Садитесь! (Do sit down), Пишите каждый день. Under negation the roles fix themselves: prohibitions are imperfective (Не опаздывай! — Don't be late), while perfective + не is a warning against an accident (Не забудь! Не упади! — Mind you don't forget/fall).
points.culture_note: Садитесь, проходите, угощайтесь — imperfective imperatives are the language of hospitality.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Grammatical aspect in Slavic languages

drills.translation: Don't be late — the lesson starts at nine.
Don't forget to take your passport!
Do sit down, please — make yourself at home.
Write me a letter when you arrive.
Read every day — and the language will come.
Careful, don't fall — the floor is wet.

points.title: Себя and сам
points.function: Point the action back at the doer, or stress who did it
points.explanation: Себя refers back to the subject and has no nominative: Он думает только о себе (about himself), Она купила себе платье (for herself), Посмотри на себя. Сам stresses that the subject did it personally and agrees like an adjective: Она сама это сделала (she did it herself), Я сам виноват.
points.culture_note: Чувствуйте себя как дома — 'feel yourself at home' — is the standard welcome formula.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: себя (Russian)

drills.translation: He thinks only about himself.
She bought herself a new dress.
She did it herself, without help.
Look at yourself in the mirror.
It's my own fault — sorry (male speaker).
Take water with you — it will be hot.

points.title: Present active participles (-ущий/-ющий)
points.function: Describe someone by what they are doing
points.explanation: Take the они-form, drop -т, add -щий: читают → читающий (who is reading), живут → живущий, говорят → говорящий. It declines like an adjective and agrees with its noun: студенты, живущие в Москве (students living in Moscow). A bookish equivalent of который + present: человек, читающий газету = человек, который читает газету.
points.culture_note: Participles belong to written and formal Russian — in speech, который does the job.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — participles

drills.translation: The man reading the newspaper is my neighbour.
drills.hint: to read + present active participle, masculine

drills.sentence: Студенты, {{answer}} в Москве, часто ходят в театры.
drills.translation: Students living in Moscow often go to the theatre.
drills.transliteration: Studenty', ___ v Moskve, chasto hodyat v teatry'.

drills.translation: The woman talking on the phone is our director.
The children playing in the park are very noisy.
Everyone who speaks Russian can take part.

drills.sentence: Поезд, {{answer}} в Москву, отходит в шесть.
drills.translation: The train going to Moscow leaves at six.
drills.hint: to go + present active participle, masculine
drills.transliteration: Poezd, ___ v Moskvu, othodit v shest'.

points.title: Past active participles (-вший)
points.function: Describe someone by what they did
points.explanation: Drop -л from the past tense and add -вший: прочитал → прочитавший (who read/has read), живший (who lived), написавший. Agrees like an adjective: писатель, живший в Париже (the writer who lived in Paris). Equivalent to который + past: студент, сдавший экзамен = студент, который сдал экзамен.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — participles

drills.sentence: Писатель, {{answer}} в Париже, вернулся в Россию.
drills.translation: The writer who had lived in Paris returned to Russia.
drills.transliteration: Pisatel', ___ v Parizhe, vernulsya v Rossiyu.

drills.translation: The student who passed the exam was very glad.
drills.hint: to pass (an exam) + past active participle

drills.translation: Everyone who has read this book recommends it.
The woman who wrote this letter is a well-known journalist.
The guests who arrived yesterday have already left.
The person who opened the window apologised.
drills.hint: to open + past active participle, masculine

points.title: Past passive participles (-нный/-тый)
points.function: Describe things by what was done to them
points.explanation: Formed from perfective verbs: написать → написанный (written), прочитать → прочитанный, построить → построенный, закрыть → закрытый. Agrees like an adjective: письмо, написанное вчера (a letter written yesterday); дом, построенный в прошлом году. The doer, if mentioned, takes the instrumental: роман, написанный Толстым.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — participles

drills.translation: The letter written yesterday has already arrived.

drills.sentence: Дом, {{answer}} в прошлом году, уже продан.
drills.translation: The house built last year has already been sold.
drills.hint: to build + past passive participle, masculine
drills.transliteration: Dom, ___ v proshlom godu, uzhe prodan.

drills.translation: This is a novel written by Tolstoy.

drills.sentence: Книга, {{answer}} мной летом, лежит на полке.
drills.translation: The book I read in the summer is lying on the shelf.
drills.transliteration: Kniga, ___ mnoj letom, lezhit na polke.

drills.sentence: Дверь, {{answer}} на ключ, никто не открыл.
drills.translation: Nobody opened the door locked with a key.
drills.transliteration: Dver', ___ na klyuch, nikto ne otkry'l.

drills.translation: All tickets bought online are cheaper.

points.title: Short passive participles (дом построен)
points.function: Report results and states — the Russian passive
points.explanation: The short form of the past passive participle is the everyday passive: Дом построен (The house has been built), Письмо написано, Работа сделана, Магазин закрыт. It agrees in gender and number: закрыт/закрыта/закрыто/закрыты. Past and future add быть: Работа была сделана вовремя. Магазин будет закрыт завтра.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — participles

drills.translation: The shop is closed — come back tomorrow.
drills.hint: to close + short passive form, masculine

drills.translation: The work is already done.
The letter is written, it can be sent.
This house was built a hundred years ago.
The tickets are already bought — we're going to the theatre!
The door was open all night.

points.title: Imperfective gerund (читая)
points.function: Describe two actions happening at once
points.explanation: The imperfective gerund (-я/-а from the present stem: читая, говоря, слушая) means 'while doing': Читая книгу, я делаю заметки (While reading, I take notes). The gerund's hidden subject must be the sentence subject. Reflexives keep -сь: занимаясь, улыбаясь.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — adverbial participles

drills.translation: While reading a book, I take notes.
Strolling through the park, we talked.
She listens to music while cooking dinner.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} с нами, он всё время улыбался.
drills.translation: While talking with us, he smiled the whole time.
drills.transliteration: ___ s nami, on vsyo vremya uly'balsya.

drills.translation: When doing sports, watch your breathing.
He works while listening to the radio.

points.title: Perfective gerund (прочитав)
points.function: Say what happened before the main action
points.explanation: The perfective gerund (-в from the past stem: прочитав, написав, купив) means 'having done': Прочитав книгу, я вернул её в библиотеку (Having read the book, I returned it). Prefixed motion verbs use -я: придя домой (having come home). Reflexives take -вшись: вернувшись, умывшись.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — adverbial participles

drills.sentence: {{answer}} книгу, я вернул её в библиотеку.
drills.translation: Having read the book, I returned it to the library.
drills.transliteration: ___ knigu, ya vernul eyo v biblioteku.

drills.translation: Having written the letter, she went to the post office.
Having come home, he had dinner right away.
Having bought the tickets, we went into the hall.
Having returned home, she called her mum.
Having finished the work, he switched off the computer.

points.title: Verbal prefixes (пере-, до-, за-)
points.function: Build precise new verbs from ones you know
points.explanation: Prefixes carry meanings across the whole verb system: пере- = again or across (перечитать — reread, переписать — rewrite), до- = up to the end (дочитать до конца — read to the end), за- = start or drop in (записать — write down, зайти — pop in), раз-/рас- = apart or undo (рассказать — tell, раздать — hand out). Learn the prefix meanings once and hundreds of verbs decode themselves.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: пере- (Russian)

drills.sentence: Книга так понравилась, что я хочу её {{answer}}.
drills.translation: I liked the book so much that I want to reread it.
drills.transliteration: Kniga tak ponravilas', chto ya hochu eyo ___.

drills.sentence: Я {{answer}} книгу до конца за одну ночь.
drills.translation: I read the book to the end in one night.
drills.hint: до- = up to the end, past masculine
drills.transliteration: Ya ___ knigu do koncza za odnu noch'.

drills.translation: Write down my phone number, please.

drills.sentence: В тексте много ошибок — надо его {{answer}}.
drills.translation: There are many mistakes in the text — it needs to be rewritten.
drills.transliteration: V tekste mnogo oshibok — nado ego ___.

drills.translation: Tell us about your trip!

drills.sentence: По дороге домой я {{answer}} в магазин.
drills.translation: On the way home I popped into the shop.
drills.transliteration: Po doroge domoj ya ___ v magazin.

points.title: Negative pronouns (никто, ничего)
points.function: Say that nobody, nothing, or never applies
points.explanation: Negative pronouns REQUIRE не on the verb — double negation is obligatory: Никто не пришёл (Nobody came), Я ничего не знаю (I don't know anything), Он никогда не был в Москве. In case forms a preposition splits the word: ни у кого, ни о чём.
points.culture_note: Ничего is also the universal shrug — 'it's fine, no matter': Как дела? — Ничего.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Double negative — Slavic languages

drills.translation: Nobody came to the meeting.
I don't know anything about this.
He has never been to Moscow.
Has nobody seen my phone?

drills.sentence: Мы {{answer}} не нашли в этом магазине.
drills.translation: We didn't find anything in this shop.
drills.transliteration: My' ___ ne nashli v e'tom magazine.

drills.sentence: Она {{answer}} не рассказала о своих планах.
drills.translation: She didn't tell anyone about her plans.
drills.transliteration: Ona ___ ne rasskazala o svoih planah.

points.title: Participial style: replacing который
points.function: Write compact formal prose
points.explanation: Written Russian compresses который-clauses into participial phrases: студент, который сдал экзамен → студент, сдавший экзамен; вопросы, которые обсуждаются на собрании → вопросы, обсуждаемые на собрании. Reflexive verbs give -щийся participles: дом, который строится → строящийся дом. Mastering the transformation in both directions is the key reading skill for newspapers and academic texts.
points.culture_note: Official and academic Russian stacks participles heavily; spoken Russian avoids them almost entirely. Register is the point.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — participles

drills.translation: The questions being discussed at the meeting were important.

drills.sentence: Рядом стоит {{answer}} дом — через год он будет готов.
drills.translation: Nearby stands a house under construction — in a year it will be ready.
drills.hint: 'which is being built' as one participle
drills.transliteration: Ryadom stoit ___ dom — cherez god on budet gotov.

drills.translation: The measures taken by the government produced results.
The documents requiring a signature are on the desk.
All available tickets have already been sold.

drills.sentence: Учёные, {{answer}} эту проблему, собрались в Москве.
drills.translation: The scholars studying this problem gathered in Moscow.
drills.transliteration: Uchyony'e, ___ e'tu problemu, sobralis' v Moskve.

points.title: General-factual imperfective (Вы читали…?)
points.function: Ask and state whether something ever happened
points.explanation: When only the FACT of an action matters — not its result or completion — Russian uses the imperfective even for finished events: Вы читали «Войну и мир»? (Have you ever read War and Peace?), Я уже смотрел этот фильм (I've seen it before). Asking who performed an activity also takes the imperfective: Кто писал этот отчёт? Result-focused contexts keep the perfective: Ты прочитал мой отчёт? (Did you finish it — I need it now.)

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Grammatical aspect in Slavic languages

drills.translation: Have you read War and Peace? — Yes, long ago.

drills.sentence: Я уже {{answer}} этот фильм, давай другой.
drills.translation: I've already seen this film, let's pick another.
drills.transliteration: Ya uzhe ___ e'tot fil'm, davaj drugoj.

drills.sentence: Кто {{answer}} этот отчёт? У меня есть вопросы.
drills.translation: Who wrote this report? I have some questions.
drills.transliteration: Kto ___ e'tot otchyot? U menya est' voprosy'.

drills.translation: Have you ever tried borscht?

drills.sentence: Мы уже {{answer}} эту оперу, в прошлом сезоне.
drills.translation: We've already heard this opera, last season.
drills.transliteration: My' uzhe ___ e'tu operu, v proshlom sezone.

drills.sentence: Ты {{answer}} мой отчёт до конца? Он нужен мне сейчас.
drills.translation: Did you finish reading my report? I need it now.
drills.transliteration: Ty' ___ moj otchyot do koncza? On nuzhen mne sejchas.

points.title: Чтобы + past: wishes and demands
points.function: Say what you want someone else to do
points.explanation: When the wanter and the doer are different people, чтобы takes a PAST-tense verb (the Russian subjunctive): Я хочу, чтобы ты пришёл (I want you to come), Она попросила, чтобы мы подождали. The same pattern follows надо, важно, нельзя: Важно, чтобы все были готовы. Compare same-subject purpose, which keeps the infinitive: Я пришёл, чтобы поговорить.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: чтобы (Russian)

drills.translation: I want you to come tomorrow (to a male).

drills.sentence: Она попросила, чтобы мы {{answer}} её у входа.
drills.translation: She asked us to wait for her at the entrance.
drills.transliteration: Ona poprosila, chtoby' my' ___ eyo u vhoda.

drills.sentence: Важно, {{answer}} все были готовы к девяти.
drills.translation: It is important that everyone be ready by nine.
drills.transliteration: Vazhno, ___ vse by'li gotovy' k devyati.

drills.translation: The teacher wants the students to read more.
The parents want their daughter to become a doctor.
Tell him not to be late.

points.title: Reflexive passive (дом строится)
points.function: Describe processes without naming the doer
points.explanation: Imperfective verbs form their passive with -ся: Дом строится уже два года (has been under construction), Журнал продаётся везде (is sold everywhere), Вопрос обсуждается (is being discussed). The doer, when named, takes the instrumental: Проект разрабатывается нашей командой. This is the imperfective counterpart of the short passive participle (дом построен).
points.culture_note: Shop signs live on this construction: Продаётся квартира. Сдаётся комната.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: -ся (Russian)

drills.translation: The new school has been under construction for two years.
This magazine is sold at any kiosk.
This question is now being discussed at the meeting.
The project is being developed by our team.

drills.sentence: Эта книга легко {{answer}} за один вечер.
drills.translation: This book reads easily in one evening.
drills.transliteration: E'ta kniga legko ___ za odin vecher.

drills.translation: The exams are held in June.

points.title: Declension of complex numerals
points.function: Handle numbers inside case constructions
points.explanation: Numerals themselves decline: около пятидесяти человек (about fifty people — genitive), с пятьюдесятью рублями (with fifty roubles — instrumental), более двухсот студентов (more than two hundred). In tens both halves decline (пятьдесят → пятидесяти → пятьюдесятью); сорок, девяносто and сто simply take -а in every oblique case: около сорока, около ста.
points.culture_note: Even educated native speakers stumble over пятьюдесятью in fast speech — polished oblique numerals are a mark of formal fluency.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian declension — numerals

drills.translation: There were about fifty people in the hall.
More than two hundred students passed the exam.
He is about forty years old.
This building is more than a hundred years old.

drills.sentence: Он вышел из дома с {{answer}} рублями в кармане.
drills.translation: He left the house with fifty roubles in his pocket.
drills.transliteration: On vy'shel iz doma s ___ rublyami v karmane.

drills.sentence: Автобус ехал со скоростью около {{answer}} километров в час.
drills.translation: The bus was travelling at about sixty kilometres per hour.
drills.transliteration: Avtobus ehal so skorost'yu okolo ___ kilometrov v chas.

points.title: Formal participial and nominal style
points.function: Read and produce official, academic Russian
points.explanation: Formal Russian layers participles and verbal nouns where speech would use clauses: в сложившейся ситуации (in the situation that has developed), принятие решения (the taking of a decision), при рассмотрении вопроса (when considering the question). Reflexive past participles (сложившийся, оставшийся) and nominalizations in -ние/-тие (изучение, развитие, принятие) are the backbone of this register.
points.culture_note: Bureaucratic Russian (канцелярит) is famous enough to have a name — Korney Chukovsky coined it while campaigning against the style. Learners still must read it: contracts, regulations, and news all run on it.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Russian grammar — participles

drills.translation: In the situation that has developed, one must act quickly.
The taking of the decision took two months.
Difficulties arose during the consideration of the question.
The remaining time is best used for preparation.
The study of a language requires daily practice.
The parties signed an agreement on the development of cooperation.

points.title: Aspect nuance: annulled results (открывал vs открыл)
points.function: Signal whether a result still holds
points.explanation: With verbs whose action can be undone, the past imperfective implies the result was CANCELLED: Кто-то открывал окно (someone opened the window — it's closed now) vs Кто-то открыл окно (it's still open). К нам приезжал брат (my brother visited — he's gone) vs приехал (he's here). Он брал мою книгу (borrowed and returned it) vs взял (still has it). The choice quietly reports the current state of the world.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wikipedia: Grammatical aspect in Slavic languages

drills.sentence: Здесь холодно — кто-то {{answer}} окно, но уже закрыл.
drills.translation: It's cold in here — someone had the window open, but has already closed it.
drills.transliteration: Zdes' holodno — kto-to ___ okno, no uzhe zakry'l.

drills.sentence: К нам {{answer}} брат — жаль, ты его не застал.
drills.translation: My brother visited us — a pity you missed him.
drills.transliteration: K nam ___ brat — zhal', ty' ego ne zastal.

drills.sentence: Он {{answer}} мою книгу и до сих пор не вернул.
drills.translation: He took my book and still hasn't returned it.
drills.transliteration: On ___ moyu knigu i do sih por ne vernul.

drills.sentence: Она {{answer}} мою ручку, но уже положила на место.
drills.translation: She borrowed my pen, but has already put it back.
drills.transliteration: Ona ___ moyu ruchku, no uzhe polozhila na mesto.

drills.sentence: Кто {{answer}} свет? В комнате темно было, а теперь горит.
drills.translation: Who turned on the light? The room was dark, and now it's on.
drills.transliteration: Kto ___ svet? V komnate temno by'lo, a teper' gorit.

drills.sentence: Ко мне {{answer}} гости, поэтому посуда не убрана.
drills.translation: I had guests over, which is why the dishes aren't cleared.
drills.hint: they came and left → imperfective plural
drills.transliteration: Ko mne ___ gosti, poe'tomu posuda ne ubrana.

points.title: Discourse particles (же, ведь, -то)
points.function: Colour a sentence with attitude and shared knowledge
points.explanation: Particles carry the tone natives take for granted. Же marks impatience or obviousness: Я же тебе говорил! (I TOLD you!). Ведь appeals to what both speakers know: Ты ведь знаешь его (you know him, after all). Enclitic -то highlights the topic, often with a contrast lurking: Денег-то у него нет (money is exactly what he doesn't have).
points.culture_note: Particles are why literal translations of Russian dialogue feel flat — the attitude lives in these little words, not in the verbs.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: же (Russian)

drills.sentence: Я {{answer}} тебе говорил, что магазин закрыт!
drills.translation: I told you the shop was closed!
drills.transliteration: Ya ___ tebe govoril, chto magazin zakry't!

drills.sentence: Ты {{answer}} знаешь его — он никогда не опаздывает.
drills.translation: You know him, after all — he is never late.
drills.transliteration: Ty' ___ znaesh' ego — on nikogda ne opazdy'vaet.

drills.translation: Do come — it's a holiday today, after all.

drills.sentence: Где {{answer}} ты был? Мы ждали два часа!
drills.translation: Where on earth were you? We waited two hours!
drills.transliteration: Gde ___ ty' by'l? My' zhdali dva chasa!

drills.sentence: Он обещал помочь, а денег-{{answer}} у него нет.
drills.translation: He promised to help, but money is just what he doesn't have.
drills.transliteration: On obeshhal pomoch', a deneg-___ u nego net.

drills.translation: Just do it today, don't put it off.

points.title: Emphatic focus: именно and это-clefts
points.function: Put the spotlight on one element of the sentence
points.explanation: Именно pins the focus: Именно он мне помог (it was HE who helped me), именно поэтому (that is exactly why). Это in front of a question word builds the Russian cleft: Это кто звонил? (Who was it that called?). Both let you contradict and correct precisely: Мне нужна именно эта книга, а не другая.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
Wiktionary: именно (Russian)

drills.sentence: {{answer}} он помог мне, когда все отказались.
drills.translation: It was he who helped me when everyone else refused.
drills.transliteration: ___ on pomog mne, kogda vse otkazalis'.

drills.sentence: Мне нужна {{answer}} эта книга, а не другая.
drills.translation: It's precisely this book I need, not another one.
drills.transliteration: Mne nuzhna ___ e'ta kniga, a ne drugaya.

drills.translation: That is exactly why I called you.
Who was it that called so late?

drills.sentence: {{answer}} здесь мы встретились в первый раз.
drills.translation: It was right here that we met for the first time.
drills.transliteration: ___ zdes' my' vstretilis' v pervy'j raz.

drills.translation: What exactly do you want to say?
